---
title: "Architecture"
subtitle: "How Dittonomics keeps the upstream pipeline logic while changing the implementation layer"
---

# System Overview

The upstream architecture Dittonomics builds from is powerful because it separates:

- project constitution
- always-needed workflow rules
- on-demand references
- on-demand workflows
- specialist agents
- infrastructure behavior

The Codex adaptation keeps that shape, but translates the layers into Codex-native surfaces.

| Layer | Codex-native location | Loaded when |
|---|---|---|
| Session constitution | `AGENTS.md` | every session in the repo |
| Repo overlay | `AGENTS.override.md` | every session when present |
| Repo-local skills | `.agents/skills/` | on demand or by explicit mention |
| Forkable starter bundle | `.codex/` in this repo | when a user copies or adapts the public starter |
| User skills | `~/.codex/skills` | on demand or implicit by policy |
| Custom subagents | `~/.codex/agents` | when the main session delegates |
| Optional voice layer | `~/voice` | when writing or prose review needs it |
| Explicit utilities and config | scripts, references, `config.toml` | when invoked |

The important design difference is that Codex should not be documented as if hidden Claude hooks still exist. If behavior matters, it needs an explicit home.

# The Kernel Update

The public starter now exposes a stronger workflow kernel than the first site draft.

That kernel makes five things explicit:

1. phase dependencies
2. artifact contracts
3. agent families and creator-critic separation
4. routing and path-resolution rules
5. persistence ownership

In practice, that means the parent orchestrator resolves paths first, hands named targets to agents, and owns report saving unless it explicitly delegates a write target.

## Kernel references

The public `clo-workflow` starter now centers these files:

- `pipeline-contract.md`
- `artifact-contracts.md`
- `agent-registry.md`
- `routing-rules.md`

Those files are the closest thing Dittonomics has to a shared operating contract across the `clo-*` stack.

# Agent Workflow Diagram

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    YOU((You)) --> D(Discovery)
    D --> S(Strategy)
    S --> E(Execution)
    E --> W(Write)
    W --> R(Peer Review)
    R -->|score >= 95| SUB(Submission)
    E -.-> T(Talks)
    R -.->|missing lit| D
    R -.->|flawed design| S
    R -.->|more checks| E
```

## Agents by Phase

The structure remains worker -> critic:

- Discovery: librarian -> librarian_critic, explorer -> explorer_critic
- Strategy: strategist -> strategist_critic
- Execution: data_engineer or coder -> coder_critic
- Writing: writer -> writer_critic
- Peer Review: editor plus two blind referees
- Submission: verifier as final gate

# The Orchestrator

The main Codex session is the orchestrator.

That means:

- it reads repo guidance first
- it decides when to invoke a skill
- it dispatches subagents only for bounded work
- it resolves paths before delegation
- it owns persistence unless it deliberately hands off a named write target
- it integrates outputs and applies quality gates

The architecture explicitly rejects the idea of installing a separate orchestrator agent when the main Codex session already has that responsibility.

# Shared Task Packets

The updated public architecture now treats task packets as a first-class contract.

A good packet includes at least:

- task type and mode
- target files
- required and optional artifacts
- repo overlays
- design metadata
- output expectations
- resolved target locations
- persistence owner

This is one of the biggest practical upgrades to the public starter because it reduces ad hoc routing language between skills and subagents.

# The Worker-Critic Loop

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    W[Creator produces artifact] --> C[Critic reviews]
    C -->|score >= 80| A[Approved]
    C -->|score < 80| F[Creator revises]
    F --> C2[Critic re-reviews]
    C2 -->|pass| A
    C2 -->|round 3 fail| U[Escalate]
```

The point is not bureaucracy. It is to keep creation and evaluation separate long enough to expose weak arguments before they become expensive.

# Voice as a Layer, Not a Hidden Habit

The live system now treats writing voice as a modular layer.

That matters because it lets users keep prose style explicit without hardcoding a private writing voice into every generic starter file. In the public repo, the voice layer is optional and documented through templates rather than copied from any one installation.

# Exploration and File Promotion

`explorations/` is still part of the architecture, not a convenience folder. Prototype work should start there and only move into canonical outputs once it stabilizes.

Promotion decisions still belong to the orchestrator or the user, not to critics.

# Why This Is Not a Literal Clone

The site is intentionally similar to Clo-Author at the workflow level, but not at the implementation label level. The manual should help a Codex user operate the system correctly, not cosmetically.
